meta

meta is a deterministic metadata backend for defining structured models once and carrying them across XML, SQL, and C# without semantic drift. XML workspaces provide a git-friendly authoring surface, while generated SQL and C# provide native relational and application surfaces over the same model and instance graph.

Foundation command-line tools

The foundation is delivered through five command-line tools:

  • meta authors and transforms model and instance workspaces, performs model-aware inspection and refactoring, imports external representations, and generates SQL and C# consumables.
  • meta-cli models command grammars and supplies the shared parser, help, workspace-loading, and handler runtime used by the suite.
  • meta-weave defines and validates sanctioned property bindings across models, then materializes those bindings when one combined workspace is needed.
  • meta-docs combines generated reference facts with authored technical documentation, then merges and renders the public reference.
  • meta-mesh declares the workspaces involved in a repository workflow and the ordered executable steps that operate on them.

BI-specific sanctioned models and command-line tools live in the separate meta-bi repository.

Metadata foundations

In this project, metadata is not an annotation around the product. Metadata is the product model itself.

  • model.xml declares the schema contract: entities, scalar properties, and relationships.
  • instances/*.xml carries the instance graph for that model: records, values, and relationship usages.
  • workspace.xml records workspace-level representation and storage configuration.

Core terms:

  • Model: a named set of entity definitions.
  • Entity: a record type, comparable to a table or a POCO type.
  • Property: a scalar attribute on an entity instance.
  • Relationship: a modeled reference from one entity to another.
  • Instance: one identified record of an entity.
  • Workspace: an on-disk unit containing a model, its instances, and workspace configuration.

Why isomorphic metadata

Isomorphic metadata preserves the same structure and meaning across its supported representations instead of translating each representation into a different product model.

One model can therefore have three native working surfaces, each suited to a different kind of work:

  • XML workspace: deterministic, reviewable, and git-friendly for authoring and version control.
  • SQL: relational and database-native, with keys and constraints carrying integrity where data platforms already work.
  • C#: application-native, with strongly typed objects and object references for tools, services, and generated consumers.

Because the semantics remain aligned, different producers and consumers can collaborate without rebuilding the model at every boundary:

  • analysts and modelers can author and review workspace data;
  • platform engineers can consume SQL and SQL-project outputs;
  • application and tooling engineers can consume generated C# APIs.

Why Git matters for metadata

A workspace is designed to behave well under version control:

  • deterministic writer output keeps diffs stable and reviewable;
  • model and instance changes can be reviewed with the same discipline as source code;
  • branch and merge workflows can carry metadata evolution;
  • refactors remain auditable through before-and-after workspace changes.

The XML writer uses stable ordering, UTF-8 without a byte-order mark, and LF line endings so representation noise does not obscure semantic changes.

Workspace contract

A workspace is a directory with this baseline shape:

workspace.xml
model.xml
instances/...

Instance data may be sharded across several files. Loading combines rows for the same entity; saving preserves the existing shard layout and writes new rows to that entity's primary shard.

Meta CLI at a glance

meta follows the working lifecycle of a metadata workspace:

  • Create and inspect: initialize workspaces and inspect their current model and instance state (init, status).
  • Explore: list, view, query, and traverse modeled data (list, view, query, graph).
  • Author: change model definitions and instance data through model-aware commands (model ..., insert, delete, bulk-insert, instance ...).
  • Refactor: analyze candidate relationships and apply atomic model-plus-instance changes (model suggest, model refactor ...).
  • Exchange representations: import source material and generate SQL, C#, and other sanctioned consumables (import ..., generate ...).

A typical flow starts with a model and instance workspace, uses meta to preserve and transform that modeled truth, then emits the native artifacts needed by databases, applications, and downstream generators.

Model XML contract

model.xml declares the schema of a metadata workspace: which entities exist, which scalar properties they carry, and how they relate.

A model has one <Model name="..."> root and follows a small set of rules:

  • <Entity name="Cube"> defines a record type; every entity has an implicit Id.
  • <PropertyList> contains scalar fields. String is the default data type, properties are required by default, and isRequired="false" makes a property optional.
  • <RelationshipList> contains references to other entities. Relationships are required by default.
  • A relationship role is used when more than one relationship targets the same entity and the references need distinct meanings.
  • XML instance containers and generated C# root collections use the deterministic <EntityName>List convention.

The model contract is intentionally small. Meaning that must survive generation belongs in entities, properties, and relationships rather than in parser conventions or surrounding prose.

Instance XML contract

instances/*.xml carries the data for a model.

  • The document root is the model name, such as <EnterpriseBIPlatform>.
  • Each entity's records appear under a deterministic list container such as <CubeList> or <MeasureList>.
  • Each record uses the singular entity name and has an Id="..." attribute.
  • Relationship usages are stored as ...Id attributes. A relationship to Cube becomes CubeId; a relationship with role="SourceCube" becomes SourceCubeId.
  • Scalar properties are child elements. A missing element is unset; an empty element is an explicitly empty string.

These XML conventions are deterministic representation rules. The loaded C# model still carries relationships as object references, while generated SQL carries them as relational keys.

Install and run

Build the foundation and weave solutions with .NET 8:

dotnet build Metadata.Framework.sln
dotnet build MetaWeave.sln

Run a CLI directly from its project during development:

dotnet run --project Meta/Cli/Meta.Cli.csproj -- help

When the CLI output directory is on PATH, use the executable name directly:

meta help

On Windows, the meta, meta-weave, and meta-docs project builds also refresh their framework-dependent publish directories under Cli\bin\publish\win-x64.

Build the offline installer package with:

powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File Meta\Installer\package-offline.ps1

The default package is framework-dependent and requires the .NET 8 runtime on the target machine. Use -SelfContained only when the package must carry its own runtime.

Install the packaged meta, meta-weave, and meta-docs CLIs into %LOCALAPPDATA%\meta\bin with:

Meta\Installer\bin\publish\win-x64-framework-dependent-shared\install-meta.exe

Command guide

Workspace-oriented commands use the current working directory when --workspace is omitted. They do not search parent directories. Use --workspace <path> when the target workspace is elsewhere.

Common command behavior:

  • successful state changes print Ok;
  • usage and runtime failures explain what prevented the command from continuing and return a non-zero exit code;
  • mutating commands that expose --strict promote warnings to errors.

Discover the command surface

meta help
meta help model
meta model suggest --help

Top-level help lists command groups. Command help then narrows the surface to the relevant usage, arguments, and options.

Internal packages

The foundation libraries are available as internal NuGet packages:

  • Meta.Core
  • Meta.Adapters
  • MetaWeave.Core

Build them into the local .nupkg feed with:

.\pack-internal.cmd

Downstream repositories consume packages rather than project references back into this repository. That keeps the foundation boundary explicit and prevents downstream changes from silently reaching into core source.

meta-bi currently consumes Meta.Core from this feed. MetaWeave.Core is published alongside it for consumers that need sanctioned cross-model binding services.

Examples

Create a workspace and emit SQL/C#

Create a workspace, add a small model and one row, then emit SQL and C# outputs from the same model and instance graph.

meta init .\Workspace
cd .\Workspace
meta model rename-model MetadataModel WorkspaceCatalog
meta model add-entity System
meta model add-property System SystemName --required true
meta insert System 1 --set "SystemName=Enterprise Analytics Platform"
meta status
meta generate sql --out ..\out\sql
meta generate csharp --out ..\out\csharp --tooling
One sample across XML, SQL, and C#

The same EnterpriseBIPlatform sample can be represented as workspace XML, emitted as SQL, and consumed through generated C# tooling.

XML model (model.xml)

`model.xml` defines the schema for your metadata workspace: which entities exist, what properties they have, and how entities relate to each other.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<Model name="EnterpriseBIPlatform">
  <EntityList>
    <Entity name="Cube">
      <PropertyList>
        <Property name="CubeName" />
        <Property name="Purpose" isRequired="false" />
        <Property name="RefreshMode" isRequired="false" />
      </PropertyList>
    </Entity>

    <Entity name="Measure">
      <PropertyList>
        <Property name="MeasureName" />
        <Property name="MDX" isRequired="false" />
      </PropertyList>
      <RelationshipList>
        <Relationship entity="Cube" />
      </RelationshipList>
    </Entity>

    <Entity name="SystemType">
      <PropertyList>
        <Property name="TypeName" />
      </PropertyList>
    </Entity>

    <Entity name="System">
      <PropertyList>
        <Property name="SystemName" />
        <Property name="Version" isRequired="false" />
      </PropertyList>
      <RelationshipList>
        <Relationship entity="SystemType" />
      </RelationshipList>
    </Entity>

    <Entity name="SystemCube">
      <PropertyList>
        <Property name="ProcessingMode" isRequired="false" />
      </PropertyList>
      <RelationshipList>
        <Relationship entity="Cube" />
        <Relationship entity="System" />
      </RelationshipList>
    </Entity>
  </EntityList>
</Model>
XML instance (instances/*.xml)

`instances/*.xml` stores the data for a model. The root element is the model name, each entity's instances are grouped under a deterministic list container element, and relationships are stored as `...Id` attributes on the record.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<EnterpriseBIPlatform>
  <CubeList>
    <Cube Id="1">
      <CubeName>Sales Performance</CubeName>
      <Purpose>Monthly revenue and margin tracking.</Purpose>
      <RefreshMode>Scheduled</RefreshMode>
    </Cube>
  </CubeList>

  <MeasureList>
    <Measure Id="1" CubeId="1">
      <MeasureName>Sales Amount</MeasureName>
      <MDX>[Measures].[Sales Amount]</MDX>
    </Measure>
  </MeasureList>

  <SystemTypeList>
    <SystemType Id="1">
      <TypeName>Internal</TypeName>
    </SystemType>
  </SystemTypeList>

  <SystemList>
    <System Id="1" SystemTypeId="1">
      <SystemName>Enterprise Analytics Platform</SystemName>
      <Version>2.1</Version>
    </System>
  </SystemList>

  <SystemCubeList>
    <SystemCube Id="1" CubeId="1" SystemId="1">
      <ProcessingMode>InMemory</ProcessingMode>
    </SystemCube>
  </SystemCubeList>
</EnterpriseBIPlatform>
SQL representation (meta generate sql)

`meta generate sql` emits `schema.sql` and `data.sql` as deterministic SQL consumables (DDL and INSERT scripts).

CREATE TABLE [dbo].[Cube] (
  [Id] INT IDENTITY(1,1) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  [CubeName] NVARCHAR(MAX) NOT NULL,
  [Purpose] NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL,
  [RefreshMode] NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL
);

CREATE TABLE [dbo].[Measure] (
  [Id] INT IDENTITY(1,1) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  [MeasureName] NVARCHAR(MAX) NOT NULL,
  [MDX] NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL,
  [CubeId] INT NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE [dbo].[SystemType] (
  [Id] INT IDENTITY(1,1) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  [TypeName] NVARCHAR(MAX) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE [dbo].[System] (
  [Id] INT IDENTITY(1,1) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  [SystemName] NVARCHAR(MAX) NOT NULL,
  [Version] NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL,
  [SystemTypeId] INT NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE [dbo].[SystemCube] (
  [Id] INT IDENTITY(1,1) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  [ProcessingMode] NVARCHAR(MAX) NULL,
  [CubeId] INT NOT NULL,
  [SystemId] INT NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE [dbo].[Measure]
ADD CONSTRAINT [FK_Measure_Cube_CubeId]
FOREIGN KEY ([CubeId]) REFERENCES [dbo].[Cube]([Id]);

ALTER TABLE [dbo].[System]
ADD CONSTRAINT [FK_System_SystemType_SystemTypeId]
FOREIGN KEY ([SystemTypeId]) REFERENCES [dbo].[SystemType]([Id]);

ALTER TABLE [dbo].[SystemCube]
ADD CONSTRAINT [FK_SystemCube_Cube_CubeId]
FOREIGN KEY ([CubeId]) REFERENCES [dbo].[Cube]([Id]);

ALTER TABLE [dbo].[SystemCube]
ADD CONSTRAINT [FK_SystemCube_System_SystemId]
FOREIGN KEY ([SystemId]) REFERENCES [dbo].[System]([Id]);
C# representation (meta generate csharp)

`meta generate csharp --tooling` emits a typed loaded model and model tooling classes for workspace-backed runtime use.

using EnterpriseBIPlatform;
using System;
using System.Linq;

// Tooling view: fully populated typed model loaded from workspace XML.
var model = EnterpriseBIPlatformModel.LoadFromXmlWorkspace(
    @".\Samples\Demos\EnterpriseBIPlatformTooling\Workspace");

foreach (var system in model.SystemList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{system.SystemName} [{system.SystemType.TypeName}]");

    foreach (var link in model.SystemCubeList.Where(x => x.SystemId == system.Id))
    {
        var mode = string.IsNullOrEmpty(link.ProcessingMode) ? "n/a" : link.ProcessingMode;
        Console.WriteLine($"  Cube: {link.Cube.CubeName} (mode: {mode})");
    }
}

foreach (var measure in model.MeasureList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{measure.Cube.CubeName}.{measure.MeasureName}");
}
Meta / CLI

Meta CLI

Command-line tools for the core metadata foundation.

Command-line tools

Select an item from the navigation to open its reference page.

CLISummary
meta Build deterministic metadata workspaces that can be reviewed in XML, projected into SQL, and consumed through typed C# tooling without changing the represented model.
meta-cli Author CLI command surfaces that drive shared parsing, help, and runtime binding for meta tools.
meta-docs Author, browse, merge, validate, and render modeled documentation workspaces for the meta and meta-bi suite.
meta-mesh Define repository workflows as declared workspaces and executable operation steps, then validate and run them from a model.
meta-weave Map one modeled workspace shape into another with explicit source, target, and field-level rules.
Meta / CLI

meta

Build deterministic metadata workspaces that can be reviewed in XML, projected into SQL, and consumed through typed C# tooling without changing the represented model.

Working with meta

Meta is the foundation tool for creating, evolving, inspecting, and projecting typed metadata workspaces. A workspace declares a model and its instance graph once, then makes that same structure available as deterministic XML, relational SQL, and typed C# without changing its meaning.

The CLI owns the everyday lifecycle: initialize a workspace, inspect its model and rows, make intentional edits, import a source surface, and generate a consumable representation. Model refactors rewrite the corresponding instance structure atomically, while structured diff and merge preserve stable row identities so workspace evolution can participate in normal code review and branch workflows.

The result is a durable model boundary. A database, generator, service, or analytical tool can consume a natural representation for its environment while staying connected to the same declared entities, properties, relationships, and data.

Commands

45 commands exposed by meta.

CommandSummary
meta bulk-insert Insert many instances from tsv/csv input.
meta check Check model and instance integrity.
meta delete Delete one instance.
meta deploy sqlserver Deploy SQL Server scripts.
meta export csv Export instances as CSV.
meta generate csharp Emit dependency-free POCOs and optional workspace tooling classes from the current model and instance graph.
meta generate sql Emit deterministic SQL DDL and data scripts from the workspace so the same model can be inspected and loaded through a database-native surface.
meta generate ssdt Generate SSDT artifacts.
meta graph inbound Show inbound relationships for an entity.
meta graph stats Show graph statistics.
meta help Show help.
meta import csv Import CSV data into a workspace.
meta import sql Import SQL metadata into a new workspace.
meta init Initialize a metadata workspace.
meta insert Insert one instance.
meta instance diff Compare two workspaces with the same model and write a structured change artifact over rows, scalar values, and relationship usages.
meta instance diff-aligned Diff mapped instance data using an explicit alignment workspace.
meta instance merge Apply a structured instance diff to a target workspace only when its precondition still matches, so workspace changes synchronize without text merges.
meta instance merge-aligned Apply an aligned instance diff artifact to a target workspace.
meta instance relationship list List instance relationships.
meta instance relationship set Set an instance relationship.
meta instance rename-id Rename one instance id.
meta instance update Update one instance.
meta list entities List entities in the model.
meta list properties List properties for an entity.
meta list relationships List relationships for an entity.
meta model add-entity Add an entity.
meta model add-property Add a property to an entity.
meta model add-relationship Add a relationship.
meta model drop-entity Drop an entity.
meta model drop-property Drop a property.
meta model drop-relationship Drop a relationship.
meta model refactor property-to-relationship Convert a property into a relationship.
meta model refactor relationship-to-property Convert a relationship into a property.
meta model rename-entity Rename an entity.
meta model rename-model Rename the model.
meta model rename-property Rename a property.
meta model rename-relationship Rename a relationship role.
meta model set-property-required Change whether a property is required.
meta model suggest Inspect landed scalar Id fields and print only relationship promotions with complete referential-integrity evidence.
meta query Search instances with equals/contains filters.
meta status Show workspace summary and model/instance sizes.
meta view entity View one entity definition.
meta view instance View one instance row.
meta workspace merge Merge two full workspaces into a new workspace.
meta bulk-insert Insert many instances from tsv/csv input. 7 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta bulk-insert <Entity> [--auto-id] [--file <path>] [--from tsv|csv] [--key Field[,Field2...]] [--stdin] [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

Bulk landing identity

Bulk import follows the same identity discipline as single-row insert and CSV import: row identity is explicit, stable, and part of the workspace semantics.

When importing flat files, keep source identifiers as stable `Id` values or scalar `...Id` landing fields first. Relationship promotion should happen after the landed workspace has complete referential-integrity evidence.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--auto-id False
--file <path> <path> False
--from tsv|csv tsv|csv False
--key Field[,Field2...] Field[,Field2...] False
--stdin False
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

meta generate csharp Emit dependency-free POCOs and optional workspace tooling classes from the current model and instance graph. 4 options / 2 examples

Usage

meta generate csharp --out <dir> [--strict] [--tooling] [--workspace <path>]

Generated C# surfaces

`meta generate csharp` emits dependency-free consumer types in a namespace that matches the model name (for example `namespace EnterpriseBIPlatform`):

- `<ModelName>.cs` (static model facade with a built-in instantiated snapshot, e.g. `EnterpriseBIPlatform`) - `<Entity>.cs` (one file per entity, POCOs)

If you also run `meta generate csharp --tooling`, `meta` emits a typed loaded model plus `<ModelName>.Tooling.cs` in the same model-name namespace. This tooling path is for workspace-backed runtime use; it does not emit the static built-in facade used by the dependency-free consumer path.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--out <dir> <dir> False
--strict False
--tooling False
--workspace <path> <path> False

Examples

Generate typed workspace tooling

Use the tooling form when another CLI, service, or generator needs to load a workspace and work with strongly typed model objects.

meta generate csharp --workspace .\Workspace --out .\out\csharp --tooling
Generate C# consumer and tooling surfaces

Generate dependency-free consumer POCOs for read-only application use, or include the tooling surface when a CLI, service, or generator needs to load a workspace and work with a typed model object.

Dependency-free consumer usage
using EnterpriseBIPlatform;
using System;
using System.Linq;

// Consumer view: dependency-free POCOs + a strongly-typed model facade.
foreach (var system in EnterpriseBIPlatform.SystemList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{system.SystemName} [{system.SystemType.TypeName}]");

    foreach (var link in EnterpriseBIPlatform.SystemCubeList.Where(x => x.SystemId == system.Id))
    {
        var mode = string.IsNullOrEmpty(link.ProcessingMode) ? "n/a" : link.ProcessingMode;
        Console.WriteLine($"  Cube: {link.Cube.CubeName} (mode: {mode})");
    }
}

foreach (var measure in EnterpriseBIPlatform.MeasureList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{measure.Cube.CubeName}.{measure.MeasureName}");
}
Optional tooling surface

The tooling view loads the workspace XML into a fully populated typed model. Use it for generators and command-line tools that need to inspect or mutate workspaces through generated C# classes.

Workspace-backed tooling usage
using EnterpriseBIPlatform;
using System;
using System.Linq;

// Tooling view: fully populated typed model loaded from workspace XML.
var model = EnterpriseBIPlatformModel.LoadFromXmlWorkspace(
    @".\Samples\Demos\EnterpriseBIPlatformTooling\Workspace");

foreach (var system in model.SystemList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{system.SystemName} [{system.SystemType.TypeName}]");

    foreach (var link in model.SystemCubeList.Where(x => x.SystemId == system.Id))
    {
        var mode = string.IsNullOrEmpty(link.ProcessingMode) ? "n/a" : link.ProcessingMode;
        Console.WriteLine($"  Cube: {link.Cube.CubeName} (mode: {mode})");
    }
}

foreach (var measure in model.MeasureList)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"{measure.Cube.CubeName}.{measure.MeasureName}");
}
meta generate sql Emit deterministic SQL DDL and data scripts from the workspace so the same model can be inspected and loaded through a database-native surface. 3 options / 1 example

Usage

meta generate sql --out <dir> [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

Generated SQL consumables

`meta generate sql` emits `schema.sql` and `data.sql` as deterministic SQL consumables.

The generated schema represents entities as tables, implicit `Id` values as primary keys, scalar properties as nullable or non-nullable columns, and required relationships as foreign-key columns plus constraints. The generated data script inserts the workspace instance rows in deterministic order.

This gives the same model and instance graph a database-native surface for inspection, interchange, and validation at scale.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--out <dir> <dir> False
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

Examples

Generate SQL consumables

Generate SQL consumables when the next stage wants relational DDL, foreign keys, and repeatable data scripts rather than XML workspace files.

meta generate sql --workspace .\Workspace --out .\out\sql
meta generate ssdt Generate SSDT artifacts. 3 options / 0 examples

meta generate ssdt --out <dir> [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

meta import csv Import CSV data into a workspace. 4 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta import csv <csvFile> --entity <EntityName> [--new-workspace <path>] [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

Id-first CSV landing

`meta import csv` is Id-first: the file must contain a column named `Id` (case-insensitive header match).

On re-import into an existing entity, matching Id updates the row, new Id inserts the row, and rows missing from the CSV are preserved. Entity containers are always `<EntityName>List`. There is no alternate id-column mapping and no best-effort reconciliation.

Scalar landing fields that point at other entities should stay as `...Id` until promoted with `meta model suggest` and `meta model refactor property-to-relationship`.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--entity <EntityName> <EntityName> False
--new-workspace <path> <path> False
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False
meta import sql Import SQL metadata into a new workspace. 4 options / 0 examples

meta insert Insert one instance. 4 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta insert <Entity> <Id> [--auto-id] [--set Field=Value] [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

Row identity

Row `Id` values are stable strings; they do not need to be numeric.

Use explicit stable `Id` values whenever the source system already has identity. `--auto-id` is only for creating brand-new rows when no external identity exists.

Refactors, diff, and merge preserve row identities. They rewrite fields and relationships; they do not remap existing rows.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--auto-id False
--set Field=Value Field=Value False
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False
meta instance diff Compare two workspaces with the same model and write a structured change artifact over rows, scalar values, and relationship usages. 2 options / 1 example

Usage

meta instance diff <leftWorkspace> <rightWorkspace> [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

What instance diff does

Workspaces evolve independently on different branches. `meta instance diff` and `meta instance merge` are deterministic synchronization primitives for instance data:

- `diff` produces a structured change artifact from Left -> Right. - `merge` applies that artifact to a target workspace with strict validation.

This is not a text diff. It is a semantic diff over entity rows, scalar values, and relationship usages.

Inputs:

- Left workspace - Right workspace

Output:

- a deterministic diff workspace artifact (default sibling path: `<RightWorkspace>.instance-diff`)

Mechanics:

- requires byte-identical `model.xml` for equal-model diff. - compares row identity by entity + `Id`. - compares scalar property values. - compares relationship usage values (`...Id` attributes). - compares row presence/absence.

Conceptual change classes captured by the artifact:

- added rows (present in Right, absent in Left) - removed rows (present in Left, absent in Right) - updated scalar values on shared rows - updated relationship references on shared rows

Determinism guarantees:

- canonical ordering/normalization is applied before writing the diff artifact. - entity/property traversal and row comparisons are stable. - same inputs produce the same logical diff artifact.

The artifact is a structured description of how to transform Left into Right.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

Examples

Move instance changes between workspaces

Use instance diff when two branches or workspaces evolved independently and you want a deterministic artifact describing the right-hand workspace changes.

meta instance diff .\BaselineWorkspace .\FeatureWorkspace
meta instance merge .\IntegrationWorkspace .\FeatureWorkspace.instance-diff
meta instance diff-aligned Diff mapped instance data using an explicit alignment workspace. 2 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta instance diff-aligned <leftWorkspace> <rightWorkspace> <alignmentWorkspace> [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False
meta instance merge Apply a structured instance diff to a target workspace only when its precondition still matches, so workspace changes synchronize without text merges. 2 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta instance merge <targetWorkspace> <diffWorkspace> [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

What instance merge does

Inputs:

- target workspace - diff artifact workspace (from `meta instance diff`)

Operation:

- verifies precondition: target must match the diff Left snapshot. - applies the diff Right snapshot to the target in memory. - normalizes and validates the resulting workspace. - persists once only if validation/postconditions pass.

Atomicity and safety:

- all changes are applied or none are persisted. - any failure restores pre-merge in-memory state and aborts. - save is a single workspace write operation after successful validation. - rows created by merge keep the `Id` values recorded in the diff artifact. - merge never allocates replacement ids or remaps existing ids.

Determinism:

- same target + same diff artifact -> same resulting workspace state.

Referential integrity and row identity

Merge is strict. It fails rather than writing invalid state.

- required relationship usage missing -> validation failure. - relationship target row missing -> validation failure. - deleting rows that remain referenced -> validation failure. - relationship updates are applied as part of the snapshot and validated with the whole workspace.

Operation ordering is snapshot-based: merge builds the target end state in memory, validates, then saves. It does not persist partial intermediate steps.

Rows are matched by identity (`Entity`, `Id`), not by value similarity.

- identity stability is assumed. - no fuzzy matching. - no business-key reconciliation. - if a target workspace has drifted so that an incoming `Id` would collide with different state, merge fails its precondition instead of remapping or guessing.

What this is not

`meta instance diff` and `meta instance merge` are not:

- text merge tools. - automatic conflict resolution. - model/schema merge. - record matching by business key. - best-effort partial reconciliation.

Typical diff/merge workflow

Example flow with two independently evolved workspaces:

1. baseline/integration workspace is your Left reference. 2. feature workspace with intended changes is your Right reference. 3. generate diff from Left -> Right. 4. apply diff into integration target (which must still match Left snapshot).

`meta instance diff .\WorkspaceA .\WorkspaceB` `meta instance merge .\WorkspaceA .\WorkspaceB.instance-diff`

If merge precondition fails, regenerate diff against the current target baseline.

`model refactor property-to-relationship` changes instance structure (scalar values moved to required relationship usages). Diff/merge captures and replays those instance-level structural value changes when both sides share the same model contract.

For model-contract drift between workspaces, use the aligned diff/merge path (`instance diff-aligned` / `instance merge-aligned`) with explicit alignment mappings.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

meta model refactor property-to-relationship Convert a property into a relationship. 7 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta model refactor property-to-relationship --lookup <Property> [--preserve-property] [--role <Role>] --source <Entity.Property> [--strict] --target <Entity> [--workspace <path>]

Atomic relationship promotion

`meta model refactor property-to-relationship` performs an atomic model+instance rewrite from a scalar property to a required relationship.

The source property is dropped by default after the relationship usage is created. Use `--preserve-property` only when the scalar field name will not collide with the implied relationship usage name.

The command preserves row identities and rewrites each affected instance from scalar `...Id` data to relationship usage data. If the workspace cannot be rewritten and validated as one complete end state, the mutation does not persist.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--lookup <Property> <Property> False
--preserve-property False
--role <Role> <Role> False
--source <Entity.Property> <Entity.Property> False
--strict False
--target <Entity> <Entity> False
--workspace <path> <path> False
meta model refactor relationship-to-property Convert a relationship into a property. 6 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta model refactor relationship-to-property [--property <PropertyName>] [--role <Role>] --source <Entity> [--strict] --target <Entity> [--workspace <path>]

Atomic relationship demotion

`meta model refactor relationship-to-property` performs the reverse structural rewrite: a required relationship usage becomes a scalar Id property.

The command preserves row identities and rewrites fields, not records. It is useful when a relationship should be flattened for interchange or when a model contract needs to move a reference back to scalar data.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--property <PropertyName> <PropertyName> False
--role <Role> <Role> False
--source <Entity> <Entity> False
--strict False
--target <Entity> <Entity> False
--workspace <path> <path> False

meta model suggest Inspect landed scalar Id fields and print only relationship promotions with complete referential-integrity evidence. 5 options / 1 example

Usage

meta model suggest [--explain] [--print-commands] [--show-keys] [--strict] [--workspace <path>]

CSV import -> suggest -> refactor

This is the intended landing workflow: import flat CSVs with stable row Ids, run suggest, then apply an atomic model+instance refactor.

`meta model suggest` is structural and deterministic. It prints strong exact-name relationship promotions by default and prints weak suggestions separately when RI is still complete but the target is only a role-style suffix match or the same source property matches more than one eligible target.

Eligible means the promotion satisfies 100% referential integrity (RI) before any mutation:

- source lands first as a scalar `...Id` field (for example `WarehouseId`) - target lookup property is complete and unambiguous (no null/blank values, no duplicates) - source property is complete (no null/blank values) - every source value resolves to an existing target lookup value (full coverage, no unmatched values) - source and target scalar types are compatible under strict rules (no implicit casting) - source values are reused, so the source behaves like a reference and the direction is structurally evidenced as many-to-one

For Id-first landing, the sanctioned structural rule is `ProductId -> Product.Id`, `SupplierId -> Supplier.Id`, and so on. `meta model suggest` does not rely on `Code` fields for this workflow. If any rule fails, the item is not printed.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--explain False
--print-commands False
--show-keys False
--strict False
--workspace <path> <path> False

Examples

Promote landing Id fields into relationships

After landing CSV files as scalar Id fields, inspect deterministic relationship suggestions and promote exact Id-to-Id references into modeled relationships.

meta import csv .\demo-csv\products.csv --entity Product --new-workspace .\Workspace
meta import csv ..\demo-csv\suppliers.csv --entity Supplier
meta import csv ..\demo-csv\categories.csv --entity Category
meta import csv ..\demo-csv\warehouses.csv --entity Warehouse
meta import csv ..\demo-csv\orders.csv --entity Order
meta model suggest --print-commands
meta model refactor property-to-relationship --source Order.ProductId --target Product --lookup Id
meta model refactor property-to-relationship --source Order.SupplierId --target Supplier --lookup Id
meta model refactor property-to-relationship --source Order.WarehouseId --target Warehouse --lookup Id

Meta / CLI

meta-cli

Author CLI command surfaces that drive shared parsing, help, and runtime binding for meta tools.

Working with MetaCli

MetaCli is the modeled command-surface language used by the meta tools. An authored workspace describes an application, its command hierarchy, executable commands, parameters, option tokens, positional arguments, value shapes, allowed values, and parameter choices as ordinary model data.

That workspace is the source for shared parsing, help generation, runtime invocation, and documentation import. A command handler binds to an executable-command identity, while the runtime resolves modeled arguments and supplies the resulting invocation. Help and usage therefore come from the same surface that the parser accepts, rather than from a separate handwritten catalog.

The authoring CLI is intentionally precise. It makes the public grammar visible before behavior is attached, so a tool can be reviewed as a command-line product in its own right and then reused consistently by people, agents, documentation, and runtime code.

Commands

16 commands exposed by meta-cli.

CommandSummary
meta-cli add-allowed-value Add one allowed value for a value shape.

meta-cli add-application-option Add an option available to all commands.

General examples

Create a small command surface

Start with a MetaCli workspace, add a command, mark it executable, then add the options the runtime should parse and document.

meta-cli new-workspace .\my-cli.MetaCli --application app-my-cli --standard-cli-shapes --default-help
meta-cli add-command --workspace .\my-cli.MetaCli --id cmd-show --application app-my-cli --name show --token show --description "Show the workspace."
meta-cli add-executable-command --workspace .\my-cli.MetaCli --id exec-show --command cmd-show
meta-cli add-option --workspace .\my-cli.MetaCli --parameter-id param-show-workspace --option-id option-show-workspace --executable-command exec-show --name workspace --value-shape shape-path --token-id token-show-workspace --token --workspace --description "Workspace path."
Meta / CLI

meta-docs

Author, browse, merge, validate, and render modeled documentation workspaces for the meta and meta-bi suite.

Working with MetaDocs

MetaDocs keeps generated reference facts and authored technical explanation in the same modeled documentation workspace. Each CLI contributes its MetaCli command surface, each product model contributes structural facts, and authored descriptions and structured examples explain the decisions that the generated facts cannot express.

Source documentation workspaces remain independent. They can be refreshed from their CLI or model source without overwriting authored prose, then merged into a suite workspace that represents the complete reference. The rendered site is a projection of that suite; it can be discarded and rebuilt at any time.

This gives the documentation a real lifecycle. Browse and search operate on the modeled reference, while update-description and the example commands author the public technical guide directly where it will be preserved across imports and regeneration.

Commands

18 commands exposed by meta-docs.

CommandSummary
meta-docs add-example Add or update a structured example for an existing documentation subject.
meta-docs add-example-code Add or update a code block in a structured documentation example.
meta-docs add-example-section Add or update a text section in a structured documentation example.
meta-docs author-page Add or update an authored documentation page in a MetaDocs workspace.
meta-docs browse Browse documentation by path.
meta-docs contents List the documentation contents as a navigable tree.
meta-docs help Show help.
meta-docs import-cli Refresh CLI reference documentation from a MetaCli workspace while preserving authored descriptions.
meta-docs import-workspace-instances Import selected workspace instance documentation from a modeled opt-in policy.
meta-docs import-workspace-model Refresh model reference documentation from a Meta workspace while preserving authored descriptions.
meta-docs include-instance-entity Add or update an opt-in entity policy for workspace instance documentation.
meta-docs include-instance-property Add or update an opt-in property policy for workspace instance documentation.

Examples

Browse CLI and model reference pages

Open the generated reference from a MetaDocs workspace, then jump to CLI, model, and search result pages by path.

cd MetaDocs\Docs\SuiteWorkspace
meta-docs browse
meta-docs browse cli/meta-docs
meta-docs browse model/MetaDocs
meta-docs search deploy

meta-docs import-cli Refresh CLI reference documentation from a MetaCli workspace while preserving authored descriptions. 6 options / 1 example

Usage

meta-docs import-cli [--application <value>] [--new-workspace <path>] [--parent-subject <value>] [--source-id <value>] --source-workspace <path> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

--parent-subject <value> Optional parent subject id for the imported CLI application. <value> False
--source-id <value> Stable documentation source id. Keep it fixed so authored descriptions stay attached. <value> False
--source-workspace <path> Source MetaCli workspace to refresh reference documentation from. <path> False
--workspace <path> Existing MetaDocs source workspace to refresh in place. <path> False

Examples

Refresh CLI reference rows

Refresh CLI reference rows from a MetaCli workspace. Keep the source id stable so authored descriptions and examples remain attached across future imports, and attach the imported application to a modeled parent subject.

meta-docs import-cli --workspace MetaDocs\Docs\Workspaces\meta-docs-cli --source-workspace MetaDocs\Cli\meta-docs.MetaCli --parent-subject public:meta:cli

Render the public docs site

Render a merged MetaDocs suite workspace into the static public reference page. The generated docs.html is disposable output; the authored source workspaces remain the durable documentation source.

meta-docs render-site --workspace MetaDocs\Docs\SuiteWorkspace --out MetaDocs\Docs\Site

Meta / CLI

meta-mesh

Define repository workflows as declared workspaces and executable operation steps, then validate and run them from a model.

Working with MetaMesh

MetaMesh turns a repository routine into a named, inspectable operation. A mesh lists the workspaces that participate and each operation records the executable steps that should occur, in order. Engineers and agents can inspect the same workspace that is later validated and run.

Workspace tokens make a step portable across a repository, while environment tokens make external configuration explicit. Before execution begins, MetaMesh resolves every declared workspace, executable, working directory, write requirement, and environment value. That full preflight gives an operation a clear prepared state before it starts changing anything.

The result is a durable replacement for task-specific wrapper scripts. Documentation regeneration, integration demos, and multi-workspace setup can be replayed as operations with visible inputs, progress, and failures that identify the exact step requiring attention.

Commands

11 commands exposed by meta-mesh.

CommandSummary
meta-mesh add-operation Add one declared operation.
meta-mesh add-step Add one executable step to an operation.
meta-mesh add-workspace Add one declared workspace.
meta-mesh help Show help.
meta-mesh new-workspace Create an empty MetaMesh workspace.
meta-mesh operations List declared operations.
meta-mesh run Run the steps declared for one operation.
meta-mesh show Show a compact mesh overview.
meta-mesh steps List steps for one operation.
meta-mesh validate Validate one operation before running it.
meta-mesh workspaces List declared workspaces.
meta-mesh add-operation Add one declared operation. 3 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-mesh add-operation [--description <value>] --name <value> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--description <value> Operation description. <value> False
--name <value> Operation name. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaMesh workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False
meta-mesh add-step Add one executable step to an operation. 10 options / 1 example

Usage

meta-mesh add-step [--arguments <arguments>] [--arguments-stdin] [--description <value>] --executable <path> [--expected-exit-code <value>] --name <value> --operation <value> [--previous-step <value>] [--working-directory <path>] [--workspace <path>]

Token substitution

Step arguments support {workspace:<name>.path}, which expands to a declared workspace path, and {env:NAME}, which verifies NAME is non-empty and passes the variable name to the child process. Use --arguments-stdin when child arguments contain quotes, SQL, or shell-sensitive text.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--arguments <arguments> Arguments passed to the executable. <arguments> False
--arguments-stdin Read executable arguments from standard input. False
--description <value> Step description. <value> False
--executable <path> Executable or script command to run. <path> False
--expected-exit-code <value> Exit code that marks the step as successful. Defaults to 0. <value> False
--name <value> Step name. <value> False
--operation <value> Operation name. <value> False
--previous-step <value> Previous step by step name. <value> False
--working-directory <path> Working directory, absolute or relative to the mesh root. <path> False
--workspace <path> MetaMesh workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

Examples

Author a step with stdin arguments

Use --arguments-stdin when the child command has quoting-heavy arguments. The step remains modeled, while the argument text stays exactly as the child CLI expects it.

@'
render-site --workspace ..\SuiteWorkspace --out ..\Site
'@ | meta-mesh add-step --operation regenerate-public-docs --name render-site --executable meta-docs --arguments-stdin --working-directory .

Preflight

Run performs validation before starting: ordered step chain, declared workspace paths, executable lookup, working-directory access, and non-empty {env:...} variables must all resolve first.

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--operation <value> Operation name. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaMesh workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

Examples

Run the documentation operation

Run a modeled operation from its mesh workspace. The documentation mesh imports source workspaces, rebuilds the suite workspace, validates it, and renders the static site.

cd MetaDocs\Docs\Documentation.MetaMesh
meta-mesh validate --operation regenerate-public-docs
meta-mesh run --operation regenerate-public-docs

Meta / CLI

meta-weave

Map one modeled workspace shape into another with explicit source, target, and field-level rules.

Working with MetaWeave

MetaWeave makes cross-model references explicit without distorting either participating model. A weave workspace records the source and target workspaces together with property bindings that explain how a scalar source value resolves to a target identity.

This lets source models retain their natural XML, SQL, and C# shape while the cross-model contract is visible, reviewable, and validated in one place. Evidence-backed suggestions help discover strong candidates, but the persisted binding remains an intentional modeled decision. Validation proves that every declared source value resolves through the binding it claims to use.

A valid weave can materialize a composed workspace when a consumer needs one model boundary. The weave itself remains the durable explanation of why that composition is correct and which source fields establish each cross-model link.

Commands

7 commands exposed by meta-weave.

CommandSummary
meta-weave add-binding Add a property binding between two model references.
meta-weave add-model Add a referenced model workspace.
meta-weave check Validate property bindings across referenced workspaces.
meta-weave help Show help.
meta-weave materialize Materialize a new workspace from a valid weave.
meta-weave new-workspace Create a MetaWeave workspace.
meta-weave suggest Suggest missing property bindings.
meta-weave add-binding Add a property binding between two model references. 8 options / 0 examples

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

meta-weave suggest Suggest missing property bindings. 1 option / 0 examples

Usage

meta-weave suggest [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--workspace <path> MetaWeave workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

General examples

Bind two workspace models and materialize

Reference source and target model workspaces, author explicit property bindings, validate them, then materialize a new workspace from the weave.

meta-weave new-workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace
meta-weave add-model --workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace --alias Source --model SourceModel --workspace-path .\Source.Workspace
meta-weave add-model --workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace --alias Target --model TargetModel --workspace-path .\Target.Workspace
meta-weave add-binding --workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace --name CustomerName --source-model Source --source-entity Customer --source-property Name --target-model Target --target-entity Customer --target-property CustomerName
meta-weave check --workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace
meta-weave materialize --workspace .\MetaWeave.Workspace --model TargetModel --new-workspace .\Materialized.Workspace

meta-bi

meta-bi is a model-driven BI engineering stack built on the generic meta foundation. It keeps source structures, SQL semantics, transformation contracts, data architecture, deployment assets, execution plans, and analytical targets as typed workspaces that can be inspected, converted, generated, and executed through one model-first foundation.

SQL as modeled structure

SQL is represented as structure rather than carried as an opaque script blob.

  • MetaTransformScript imports the supported T-SQL surface into a typed syntax graph covering statement roots, query composition, joins, expressions, predicates, grouping, windowing, functions, and bounded mutation statements. The model emits semantically equivalent SQL and proves stability through SQL -> workspace -> SQL -> workspace round trips.
  • MetaTransformBinding resolves modeled source and target identifiers against explicit MetaSchema workspaces. It records rowsets, columns, source objects, target objects, and validation links, and checks target shape and data-type compatibility through sanctioned MetaDataTypeConversion policy.
  • MetaSql carries deployable database structure, while MetaSqlDeployManifest makes source and live fingerprints, schema changes, destructive approvals, and deployment actions explicit before execution.

That shared understanding lets later layers reason from transform structure and binding evidence instead of reparsing SQL independently or guessing from text.

Sanctioned model layers

The sanctioned models divide BI concerns at their natural ownership boundaries:

  • Source and type contracts: MetaSchema captures systems, schemas, tables, fields, keys, and relationships from SQL Server. MetaDataType and MetaDataTypeConversion carry semantic types and explicit mappings into runtime type systems.
  • Data architecture: MetaRawDataVault and MetaBusinessDataVault model raw and governed vault structures. MetaDataWarehouse models dimensions, facts, grain, business keys, roles, snapshots, bridges, and slowly changing dimensions. Separate implementation workspaces own physical SQL naming, keys, platform columns, defaults, and indexes.
  • Transform and database realization: MetaTransformScript, MetaTransformBinding, MetaSql, and MetaSqlDeployManifest carry understood SQL, schema contracts, deployable objects, and controlled deployment.
  • Analytical semantics: MetaAnalytics defines portable tables, attributes, hierarchies, measures, aggregation behavior, relationships, perspectives, roles, cultures, and translations before an engine is selected.
  • Analytical targets: MetaTabular owns DAX measures, calculation groups, partitions, KPIs, and tabular security. MetaMultiDimensional owns cubes, measure groups, MDX, named sets, actions, partitions, and multidimensional security.
  • Execution and assurance: MetaDataQuality, MetaPipeline, and MetaOrchestration turn modeled transform and access evidence into reviewable checks, executable task chains, dependency graphs, synchronization policy, and runtime evidence.

meta-convert performs explicit conversions between sanctioned model surfaces, keeping portable intent separate from target-specific implementation details.

Model-driven execution and evidence

The runtime tools consume modeled evidence produced by the layers before them:

  • meta-data-quality traverses the typed transform graph to discover reviewable integrity, optionality, fanout, duplicate, join-pattern, and filter-consensus candidates. Binding evidence can restrict generation to transforms whose source and target contracts are already proven.
  • meta-pipeline executes modeled transform-backed and external-process tasks, records the exact transform and binding used, and preserves row, batch, timeout, fingerprint, audit, and failure evidence.
  • meta-orchestration derives the provable dependency graph from pipeline tasks and bound object reads and writes. Explicit ordering, lock compatibility, synchronization, and failure branches remain modeled policy instead of being buried in scheduler behavior.
  • Target CLIs realize modeled SQL, Tabular, and MultiDimensional workspaces through deployment and processing commands owned by the target model.

Automation therefore follows from explicit contracts. When additional knowledge is required, it enters as modeled policy that engineers and agents can inspect alongside the inferred evidence.

From source contracts to analytical targets

A typical path can begin with a live system extracted into MetaSchema, then move through explicit data-type policy, vault or dimensional architecture, modeled and bound SQL transforms, generated database assets, data-quality review, pipeline execution, orchestration, and a portable MetaAnalytics model projected into Tabular or MultiDimensional form.

Teams compose the sanctioned workspaces that match their architecture. At every handoff, the input and output remain inspectable model instances instead of disappearing into generator configuration or unstructured deployment scripts.

Meta-BI / CLI

Meta-BI CLI

Command-line tools for BI modeling, conversion, validation, and execution.

Command-line tools

Select an item from the navigation to open its reference page.

CLISummary
meta-analytics Author shared analytics semantics for dimensions, hierarchies, measures, facts, and security across semantic model targets.
meta-convert Convert between sanctioned BI workspaces, including schema, vault, warehouse, SQL, transform, analytics, tabular, and multidimensional models.
meta-data-quality Promote modeled transform and schema evidence into data quality views, review surfaces, and operational SQL.
meta-data-type Author sanctioned data type catalogs used by schema, SQL, conversion, and BI modeling tools.
meta-data-type-conversion Author conversion rules that map data types across platforms with explicit length, precision, scale, and fallback behavior.
meta-data-warehouse Author dimensional warehouse models and implementation policies that project to deployable SQL workspaces.
meta-datavault-business Author business Data Vault models that shape raw vault structures into governed business hubs, links, satellites, and deployable SQL.
meta-datavault-raw Author raw Data Vault structures from source schemas, including hubs, links, satellites, and deployable SQL projections.
meta-multi-dimensional Author and deploy multidimensional semantic models with cubes, dimensions, measures, partitions, actions, and MDX query support.
meta-orchestration Infer, author, and execute orchestration plans with task ordering, concurrency policy, and run evidence.
meta-pipeline Author and execute modeled pipelines with task dependencies, workspace fingerprints, SQL execution, and operational run evidence.
meta-schema Extract SQL Server schemas into modeled workspaces that preserve databases, schemas, tables, fields, keys, and relationships.
meta-sql Extract SQL Server metadata, build deploy plans, apply manifests, and execute SQL against modeled database workspaces.
meta-tabular Author and deploy tabular semantic models with tables, relationships, measures, partitions, roles, and processing commands.
meta-transform-binding Bind transform scripts to source and target schemas so references, data types, and writable contracts are verified before generation.
meta-transform-script Import SQL transform scripts into a structured model, export equivalent SQL, and manage transform contracts.
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-analytics

Author shared analytics semantics for dimensions, hierarchies, measures, facts, and security across semantic model targets.

Working with MetaAnalytics

MetaAnalytics is the portable semantic model of the BI stack. It describes data sources, tables, attributes, hierarchies, measures, facts, relationships, and security in a target-neutral form that architects and analysts can review before an analytical engine is chosen.

Analytical intent is explicit. A measure has a source and aggregation behavior, a hierarchy has a declared path, a table has a modeled purpose, and security belongs to the semantic model rather than being appended to a generated target. The result is a compact but expressive description of how the business should explore and calculate over the warehouse.

MetaConvert can project the same semantic contract into Tabular or MultiDimensional workspaces. Those target models add platform-specific capabilities while retaining the analytical design that the team agreed on here.

Commands

29 commands exposed by meta-analytics.

CommandSummary
meta-analytics add-aggregation-behavior Declare a base measure aggregate function.
meta-analytics add-attribute Add a typed table attribute or calculated attribute.
meta-analytics add-attribute-permission Add object-level security for an attribute.
meta-analytics add-attribute-relationship Declare an attribute relationship inside a table.
meta-analytics add-attribute-translation Translate attribute metadata.
meta-analytics add-culture Add a model culture.
meta-analytics add-data-source Add an analytics source declaration.
meta-analytics add-hierarchy Add a hierarchy.
meta-analytics add-hierarchy-level Add an ordered hierarchy level.
meta-analytics add-hierarchy-translation Translate hierarchy metadata.
meta-analytics add-measure Add a source-backed base measure.
meta-analytics add-measure-translation Translate measure metadata.
meta-analytics add-model Add an analytics model.
meta-analytics add-perspective Add a perspective.
meta-analytics add-perspective-attribute Expose an attribute in a perspective.
meta-analytics add-perspective-hierarchy Expose a hierarchy in a perspective.
meta-analytics add-perspective-measure Expose a measure in a perspective.
meta-analytics add-perspective-table Expose a table in a perspective.
meta-analytics add-perspective-translation Translate perspective metadata.
meta-analytics add-relationship Add a relationship between analytics tables.
meta-analytics add-role-filter Add row-level security over a table.
meta-analytics add-role-member Add a member to a security role.
meta-analytics add-security-role Add a security role.
meta-analytics add-sort-by-attribute Declare one attribute as the sort key for another.
meta-analytics add-table Add an analytics table.
meta-analytics add-table-permission Add object-level security for a table.
meta-analytics add-table-translation Translate table metadata.
meta-analytics help Show help.
meta-analytics new-workspace Create a MetaAnalytics workspace.
meta-analytics add-aggregation-behavior Declare a base measure aggregate function. 5 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-analytics add-aggregation-behavior [--description <value>] --function <value> --id <value> --measure <value> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--description <value> Description. <value> False
--function <value> Aggregate function. <value> False
--id <value> AggregationBehavior row id. <value> False
--measure <value> Measure id. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaAnalytics workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False
meta-analytics add-attribute Add a typed table attribute or calculated attribute. 17 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-analytics add-attribute [--data-category <value>] --data-type-id <value> [--description <value>] [--expression <value>] [--expression-language <value>] [--format-string <value>] --id <value> [--is-hidden true|false] [--is-key true|false] [--is-nullable true|false] [--kind <value>] --name <value> [--ordinal <value>] [--source-name <value>] [--summarize-by <value>] --table <value> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--data-category <value> Data category. <value> False
--data-type-id <value> Data type id. <value> False
--description <value> Description. <value> False
--expression <value> Expression. <value> False
--expression-language <value> Expression language. <value> False
--format-string <value> Format string. <value> False
--id <value> Attribute row id. <value> False
--is-hidden true|false Whether the attribute is hidden. true|false: false, true False
--is-key true|false Whether the attribute is a key. true|false: false, true False
--is-nullable true|false Whether the attribute is nullable. true|false: false, true False
--kind <value> Attribute kind. <value> False
--name <value> Attribute name. <value> False
--ordinal <value> Attribute ordinal. <value> False
--source-name <value> Source column name. <value> False
--summarize-by <value> Summarize by. <value> False
--table <value> Table id. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaAnalytics workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False
meta-analytics add-attribute-permission Add object-level security for an attribute. 6 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-analytics add-attribute-permission --attribute <value> [--description <value>] --id <value> --metadata-permission <value> --role <value> [--workspace <path>]

Meta-BI / CLI

meta-convert

Convert between sanctioned BI workspaces, including schema, vault, warehouse, SQL, transform, analytics, tabular, and multidimensional models.

Working with MetaConvert

MetaConvert is the projection boundary between sanctioned BI workspaces. It carries an explicit source design into the next representation: source schema into raw vault, vault or warehouse into deployable SQL, quality evidence into SQL assets, or portable analytics into Tabular and MultiDimensional targets.

Every conversion starts from modeled inputs and produces a modeled output. Where a physical implementation or deployment policy is needed, that workspace is passed explicitly, keeping logical intent separate from target-specific choices. The converter therefore preserves a reviewable chain from source contract to generated artifact.

This makes the suite composable. A team can inspect each model in its own domain, regenerate a later projection when an upstream decision changes, and avoid treating an emitted script or target project as the place where the architecture is defined.

Commands

10 commands exposed by meta-convert.

CommandSummary
meta-convert analytics-to-multi-dimensional Convert MetaAnalytics workspace to MetaMultiDimensional workspace.
meta-convert analytics-to-tabular Convert MetaAnalytics workspace to MetaTabular workspace.
meta-convert business-datavault-to-sql Convert MetaBusinessDataVault workspace to MetaSql workspace.
meta-convert data-quality-to-sql Convert promoted MetaDataQuality candidates to SQL data-quality views.
meta-convert data-warehouse-to-sql Convert MetaDataWarehouse workspace to MetaSql workspace.
meta-convert help Show help.
meta-convert raw-datavault-to-sql Convert MetaRawDataVault workspace to MetaSql workspace.
meta-convert schema-to-raw-datavault Convert MetaSchema workspace to MetaRawDataVault workspace.
meta-convert sql-to-transform-script Convert MetaSql SQL modules to MetaTransformScript workspace.
meta-convert transform-script-to-sql Convert MetaTransformScript SQL modules to MetaSql workspace.
meta-convert analytics-to-multi-dimensional Convert MetaAnalytics workspace to MetaMultiDimensional workspace. 2 options / 0 examples

meta-convert sql-to-transform-script Convert MetaSql SQL modules to MetaTransformScript workspace. 6 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-convert sql-to-transform-script [--allow-empty] [--include-functions] [--include-stored-procedures] [--include-views] --out <path> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--allow-empty Create an empty MetaTransformScript workspace when selected module kinds have no convertible modules. False
--include-functions Convert function modules. If no include switch is provided, all module kinds are selected. False
--include-stored-procedures Convert stored procedure modules. If no include switch is provided, all module kinds are selected. False
--include-views Convert view modules. If no include switch is provided, all module kinds are selected. False
--out <path> Output MetaTransformScript workspace path. <path> False
--workspace <path> Source MetaSql workspace path. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False
meta-convert transform-script-to-sql Convert MetaTransformScript SQL modules to MetaSql workspace. 3 options / 0 examples

Usage

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--database-name <value> Target MetaSql database name. <value> False
--out <path> Output MetaSql workspace path. <path> False
--workspace <path> Source MetaTransformScript workspace path. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

General examples

Derive a raw Data Vault design

Project a modeled source schema into a raw-vault workspace.

Use a MetaSchema workspace as the source contract, then create the raw-vault design that will carry its source identities and relationships forward.

meta-convert schema-to-raw-datavault --source-workspace .\SourceSchema.Workspace --new-workspace .\RawVault.Workspace
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-data-quality

Promote modeled transform and schema evidence into data quality views, review surfaces, and operational SQL.

Working with MetaDataQuality

MetaDataQuality derives reviewable quality candidates from typed transform and schema evidence. It examines the modeled transform graph, binding evidence, joins, relationships, and corpus patterns to identify missing references, uniqueness problems, unexpected null behavior, suspicious joins, and drift from repeated behavior.

Discovery is automatic because the technical basis is already modeled. Promotion remains deliberate: candidates become executable or review-oriented assets only when a user chooses to make that decision part of the workspace. The candidate, its evidence, and its promotion state stay together as inspectable data.

Generated SQL distinguishes runtime checks from semantic review findings. The resulting views and operational objects therefore preserve the reason a finding exists, support repeated execution, and give reviewers a clear route from a dashboard result back to the transform and schema evidence that produced it.

Commands

4 commands exposed by meta-data-quality.

CommandSummary
meta-data-quality from-transform-workspace Create generated DQ views from a full MetaTransformScript workspace.
meta-data-quality help Show help.
meta-data-quality inspect Review the generated DQ pack and optional adjustments.
meta-data-quality promote Promote generated DQ candidates for SQL output.
meta-data-quality from-transform-workspace Create generated DQ views from a full MetaTransformScript workspace. 3 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-quality from-transform-workspace [--binding-workspace <path>] --new-workspace <path> --transform-workspace <path>

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--binding-workspace <path> MetaTransformBinding workspace used to scan only validated scripts. <path> False
--new-workspace <path> Directory where the generated MetaDataQuality workspace will be created. <path> False
--transform-workspace <path> MetaTransformScript workspace to analyze. <path> False
meta-data-quality help Show help. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-quality help
meta-data-quality inspect Review the generated DQ pack and optional adjustments. 4 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-quality inspect [--show-candidate-ids] [--show-cases] [--top-cases <n>] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--show-candidate-ids Include candidate ids in adjustment output. Implies --show-cases. False
--show-cases Show candidate adjustment cases. False
--top-cases <n> Show up to n candidate cases. Implies --show-cases. <n> False
--workspace <path> MetaDataQuality workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False
meta-data-quality promote Promote generated DQ candidates for SQL output. 4 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-quality promote [--all] [--candidate-id <value>] [--candidate-kind <value>] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--all Promote every generated data-quality candidate. False
--candidate-id <value> Promote one generated candidate. May be provided more than once. <value> False
--candidate-kind <value> Promote generated candidates of one candidate family. May be provided more than once. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaDataQuality workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

General examples

Derive and promote quality evidence

Generate review candidates from validated transforms, then promote approved candidates.

The binding workspace limits discovery to scripts whose source and target contracts are known. Promotion marks the candidates that should become generated SQL assets.

meta-data-quality from-transform-workspace --transform-workspace .\Transforms.Workspace --binding-workspace .\Binding.Workspace --new-workspace .\DataQuality.Workspace
meta-data-quality inspect --workspace .\DataQuality.Workspace
meta-data-quality promote --workspace .\DataQuality.Workspace --all
meta-convert data-quality-to-sql --workspace .\DataQuality.Workspace --out .\DataQuality.sql
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-data-type

Author sanctioned data type catalogs used by schema, SQL, conversion, and BI modeling tools.

Working with MetaDataType

MetaDataType is the sanctioned vocabulary for types used throughout the modeled stack. A type catalog turns platform names, parameters, and identity behavior into reusable declarations instead of repeating those choices as loose strings in every schema, transform, and target.

The catalog gives downstream models a stable language for saying what a field or expression represents. Schema extraction, transform binding, warehouse design, analytics, and generated SQL can refer to the same declared type while each projection uses the natural syntax of its own platform.

That common vocabulary becomes especially valuable when a solution crosses SQL Server, analytical engines, and generated code. Type decisions stay inspectable at the model layer and conversion policy can build on them without inventing private compatibility rules.

Commands

2 commands exposed by meta-data-type.

CommandSummary
meta-data-type help Show help.
meta-data-type new-workspace Create a MetaDataType workspace.
meta-data-type help Show help. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-type help
meta-data-type new-workspace Create a MetaDataType workspace. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-data-type new-workspace <path>
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-data-type-conversion

Author conversion rules that map data types across platforms with explicit length, precision, scale, and fallback behavior.

Working with type conversion

MetaDataTypeConversion records the sanctioned paths between declared data types. A conversion states the source and target systems together with the length, precision, scale, fallback, and compatibility behavior that determine whether a value can move across a boundary.

The conversion workspace is shared policy. Transform binding uses it to evaluate source-to-target compatibility, and generators can apply the same approved behavior when they project a model into SQL or another platform. This avoids quietly embedding widening assumptions in individual tools.

Keeping conversion rules modeled also makes the hard cases visible. A design can be reviewed for loss, truncation, precision, and fallback choices before a transform or target is generated, and the resulting decision is reusable wherever the same type boundary appears.

Commands

4 commands exposed by meta-data-type-conversion.

CommandSummary
meta-data-type-conversion check Validate sanctioned type mappings.
meta-data-type-conversion help Show help.
meta-data-type-conversion new-workspace Create a seeded MetaDataTypeConversion workspace.
meta-data-type-conversion resolve Resolve one source data type id through the sanctioned mappings.

Meta-BI / CLI

meta-data-warehouse

Author dimensional warehouse models and implementation policies that project to deployable SQL workspaces.

Working with MetaDataWarehouse

MetaDataWarehouse models a dimensional relational warehouse independently of its physical SQL projection. Dimensions, attributes, business keys, facts, measures, and slowly changing behavior are declared together as one coherent contract.

The workspace provides the design surface between source or vault evidence and analytical consumption. It captures the choices that make a warehouse useful to the business: which dimensions describe a fact, how attributes identify a member, how keys behave, and where historical change is part of the design.

Implementation policy determines the deployable database shape while the warehouse model continues to express the intended dimensional structure. That separation makes the warehouse reviewable, regenerable, and suitable as a stable input to MetaAnalytics without conflating logical design with table scripts.

Meta-BI / CLI

meta-datavault-business

Author business Data Vault models that shape raw vault structures into governed business hubs, links, satellites, and deployable SQL.

Working with business Data Vault

MetaBusinessDataVault models the governed business layer built on raw Data Vault structures. It is where business-facing hubs, links, satellites, and the rules that shape raw evidence into reusable business structures are made explicit.

The workspace gives a team a stable place to review semantics that are broader than a single source system. Business identity, relationships, and descriptive context remain model data, connected to the raw layer but owned by the business design rather than hidden in downstream SQL transformations.

That explicit layer lets raw source history remain intact while business structures evolve deliberately. It can be inspected alongside the raw vault, projected through the appropriate implementation policy, and used as a controlled input to warehouse and analytical modeling.

Commands

meta-datavault-business new-workspace Create a MetaBusinessDataVault workspace. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-datavault-business new-workspace <Path>
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-datavault-raw

Author raw Data Vault structures from source schemas, including hubs, links, satellites, and deployable SQL projections.

Working with raw Data Vault

MetaRawDataVault models the source-faithful Data Vault layer. Hubs, links, satellites, source keys, and their relationships are declared as durable structures that capture identity, association, and historized source evidence.

The raw vault begins from a modeled source contract rather than a folder of database scripts. Its workspace provides the design surface between an extracted schema and a physical implementation, so the important choices remain visible before SQL is generated: which source identities form hubs, which associations form links, and which descriptive attributes belong in satellites.

The model keeps raw evidence distinct from business interpretation. That separation supports auditable ingestion, controlled regeneration, and a clear handoff to the Business Data Vault and warehouse layers without losing the connection to the systems that supplied the data.

Commands

17 commands exposed by meta-datavault-raw.

meta-datavault-raw new-workspace <path>
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-multi-dimensional

Author and deploy multidimensional semantic models with cubes, dimensions, measures, partitions, actions, and MDX query support.

Working with MetaMultiDimensional

MetaMultiDimensional models a deployable multidimensional target. Cubes, dimensions, measure groups, calculations, partitions, actions, named sets, and security are expressed as explicit structures rather than being hidden inside an SSAS project or an MDX script collection.

The workspace can be generated from MetaAnalytics when the portable semantic model is the starting point, then extended with multidimensional capabilities that belong specifically to the cube: aggregation-aware measure groups, dimension behavior, named sets, drillthrough actions, and cell-level security.

This keeps business semantics distinct from target implementation while still allowing a complete, inspectable cube design. Deployment consumes the modeled workspace, so the operational target remains tied to the same declared analytical intent.

Commands

meta-multi-dimensional deploy Create modeled multidimensional database objects, including data source views, and process unless --no-process is used; processing failures fail the command.
meta-multi-dimensional drop Drop a multidimensional database from an Analysis Services multidimensional instance with no confirmation prompt.
meta-multi-dimensional help Show help.
meta-multi-dimensional new-workspace Create a MetaMultiDimensional workspace.
meta-multi-dimensional restore Promote a processed multidimensional database through backup and restore for pre-prod-to-prod promotion; restore does not process.
