Meta-BI / CLI

meta-orchestration

Infer, author, and execute orchestration plans with task ordering, concurrency policy, and run evidence.

Working with MetaOrchestration

MetaOrchestration turns modeled pipeline tasks and bound transform access evidence into a run graph. It derives the ordering that can be proven from declared reads, writes, and effects, then represents the remaining synchronization choices as explicit policy.

That division is deliberate. Structural evidence can establish a large portion of a safe plan, while dependency rules, lock policies, and concurrent-append decisions supply the operational knowledge that only a designer can provide. The resulting workspace makes both the inferred and authored portions of orchestration visible.

Execution coordinates the modeled run plan and retains run evidence. When an issue remains unresolved, it is recorded as a specific modeled concern rather than disappearing into scheduler code or an implicit ordering convention.

Commands

12 commands exposed by meta-orchestration.

CommandSummary
meta-orchestration add-dependency Record an explicit success or failure dependency between tasks.
meta-orchestration add-order Record an explicit success dependency/order resolution in an orchestration workspace.
meta-orchestration allow-concurrent-append Allow concurrent execution for multiple Append effects on one object.
meta-orchestration execute Execute the current run plan by coordinating meta-pipeline worker processes.
meta-orchestration explain-issue Explain one analyzer issue and its participating pipelines.
meta-orchestration help Show help.
meta-orchestration infer Infer a MetaOrchestration workspace from a modeled MetaPipeline workspace.
meta-orchestration inspect Inspect a MetaOrchestration workspace.
meta-orchestration inspect-run-plan Inspect the planned task dependency graph.
meta-orchestration list-issues List analyzer issues recorded in an orchestration workspace.
meta-orchestration refresh-run-plan Refresh lock-aware run-plan rows in an orchestration workspace.
meta-orchestration set-lock-policy Record scoped lock compatibility for one object/effect interaction.
meta-orchestration add-dependency Record an explicit success or failure dependency between tasks. 6 options / 0 examples

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--condition success|failure Whether the successor follows predecessor success or failure. success|failure: failure, success False
--from-task <value> Predecessor task selector. <value> False
--object <value> Object selector for object-scoped dependency resolution. <value> False
--reason <value> Reason recorded with the policy row. <value> False
--to-task <value> Successor task selector. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaOrchestration workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--condition success|failure Dependency condition. Default: success. success|failure: failure, success False
--from-task <value> Predecessor task selector. <value> False
--object <value> Object selector for object-scoped dependency resolution. <value> False
--reason <value> Reason recorded with the policy row. <value> False
--to-task <value> Successor task selector. <value> False

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

meta-orchestration execute Execute the current run plan by coordinating meta-pipeline worker processes. 9 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-orchestration execute [--data-type-conversion-workspace <path>] [--max-degree-of-parallelism <n>] [--pipeline-db-connection-env <value>] --pipeline-workspace <path> [--run-artifacts-root <path>] [--worker-activation-timeout-seconds <n>] [--worker-control-pipe-connect-timeout-seconds <n>] [--worker-event-timeout-seconds <n>] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--data-type-conversion-workspace <path> Conversion policy workspace passed to child workers. <path> False
--max-degree-of-parallelism <n> Maximum concurrently granted pipeline tasks. Default: 1. <n> False
--pipeline-db-connection-env <value> Operational DB connection environment variable passed to child workers. <value> False
--pipeline-workspace <path> MetaPipeline workspace used by child pipeline workers. <path> False
--run-artifacts-root <path> Operational root for run journals, worker logs, and workspace execution leases. <path> False
--worker-activation-timeout-seconds <n> Startup/activation timeout. Omitted follows --worker-event-timeout-seconds; 0 disables activation timeout. <n> False
--worker-control-pipe-connect-timeout-seconds <n> Timeout while waiting for child workers to connect to the named pipe. 0 or omitted means no timeout. <n> False
--worker-event-timeout-seconds <n> Timeout for silent worker protocol periods. 0 or omitted means no timeout. <n> False
--workspace <path> MetaOrchestration workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

meta-orchestration set-lock-policy Record scoped lock compatibility for one object/effect interaction. 6 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-orchestration set-lock-policy --behavior serialize|allow --left-effect <value> --object <value> [--reason <value>] --right-effect <value> [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--behavior serialize|allow Lock behavior for the object/effect pair. serialize|allow: allow, serialize False
--left-effect <value> Left write effect, such as Append, Replace, Mutation, KeyedUpsert, or ConditionalKeyedUpsert. <value> False
--object <value> Data object whose effect interaction is being resolved. <value> False
--reason <value> Reason recorded with the policy row. <value> False
--right-effect <value> Right write effect, such as Append, Replace, Mutation, KeyedUpsert, or ConditionalKeyedUpsert. <value> False
--workspace <path> MetaOrchestration workspace. Defaults to the current directory. <path> False

General examples

Infer and review a run plan

Build orchestration from a modeled pipeline, then review the issues and plan.

Inference creates the provable part of the run graph. Any remaining ordering or lock decision is visible in the orchestration workspace before execution.

meta-orchestration infer --pipeline-workspace .\Pipeline.Workspace --new-workspace .\Orchestration.Workspace
meta-orchestration list-issues --workspace .\Orchestration.Workspace
meta-orchestration refresh-run-plan --workspace .\Orchestration.Workspace
meta-orchestration inspect-run-plan --workspace .\Orchestration.Workspace
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-pipeline

Author and execute modeled pipelines with task dependencies, workspace fingerprints, SQL execution, and operational run evidence.

Working with MetaPipeline

MetaPipeline models executable work as named pipelines and tasks. A task can be backed by a validated transform or by an explicit executable, with declared dependencies, connection context, timeouts, fingerprints, and the evidence needed to understand each run.

The workspace is the plan. It describes what should be executed and the contracts it relies on. The optional operational database records diagnostics, metrics, audits, and failures, giving an execution a useful history without becoming the source of model truth.

This makes pipeline behavior inspectable before it is scheduled. A team can author steps, review their dependencies and execution context, run a pipeline or individual task, and retain operational evidence that points back to the exact modeled plan.

Commands

12 commands exposed by meta-pipeline.

CommandSummary
meta-pipeline add-executable-step Append one executable-backed task instance to the pipeline's serial task chain.
meta-pipeline add-pipeline Add one Pipeline instance to a MetaPipeline workspace.
meta-pipeline add-step Append one transform-backed task instance to the pipeline's serial task chain.
meta-pipeline create-pipeline-db Create or update the SQL Server MetaPipeline operational DB for diagnostic logs, audit logs, metrics, and failures; it does not store model truth.
meta-pipeline execute Execute a modeled pipeline's serial task chain with compact live progress including B/KB/MB/GB rate.
meta-pipeline execute-sqlserver Execute one SQL Server transform script with script selection options and compact live progress including B/KB/MB/GB rate.
meta-pipeline execute-step Execute exactly one modeled pipeline task by task name or id; diagnostic surface, while orchestration uses execute-worker.
meta-pipeline execute-worker Execute a modeled pipeline as an orchestration worker boundary over a named pipe; stdout and stderr are diagnostics only; preserves that pipeline context and handles GrantTask and StopPipeline.
meta-pipeline help Show help.
meta-pipeline inspect Show a compact MetaPipeline workspace summary.
meta-pipeline new-workspace Create an empty sanctioned MetaPipeline workspace.
meta-pipeline prune-pipeline-db Prune old completed RunDiagnosticsLog rows while preserving RunLog and audit lineage.
meta-pipeline add-executable-step Append one executable-backed task instance to the pipeline's serial task chain. 8 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-pipeline add-executable-step [--arguments <text>] --executable <path> --pipeline <value> [--step-name <value>] [--success-exit-code <n>] [--timeout-seconds <n>] [--working-directory <path>] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--arguments <text> Raw command-line arguments passed to the executable. <text> False
--executable <path> Executable path or executable name resolvable by the operating system. <path> False
--pipeline <value> Pipeline that receives the new serial step. <value> False
--step-name <value> Step name; omitted derives a deterministic name from the executable file name. <value> False
--success-exit-code <n> Expected process exit code. Default: 0. <n> False
--timeout-seconds <n> Process timeout seconds. 0 or omitted means no timeout. <n> False
--working-directory <path> Process working directory. <path> False
--workspace <path> Existing MetaPipeline workspace to update. Defaults to the current working directory. <path> False

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

General examples

Start a modeled pipeline

Create the pipeline workspace and give the first pipeline a durable name.

Tasks and execution context are added to a named pipeline after the workspace exists. The resulting plan can then be inspected, executed, or used as orchestration input.

meta-pipeline new-workspace .\Pipeline.Workspace
meta-pipeline add-pipeline --workspace .\Pipeline.Workspace --name CustomerLoad
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-schema

Extract SQL Server schemas into modeled workspaces that preserve databases, schemas, tables, fields, keys, and relationships.

Working with MetaSchema

MetaSchema captures a live source schema as an explicit workspace. The extracted model preserves the database and system context together with schemas, tables, fields, keys, and relationships, giving every later stage a reviewable source contract.

Extraction begins from the actual SQL Server surface and lets a team choose the schemas and tables that belong in the modeled boundary. The workspace records what the source exposes with enough structure for binding, conversion, vault design, warehouse design, and contract validation to reason about it directly.

Because the schema is durable model data, it can be compared, regenerated, and cited by downstream workspaces. A transform binds to this contract; a vault or warehouse derives from it; a deployment or quality check can state exactly which source objects it relies on.

Commands

2 commands exposed by meta-schema.

CommandSummary
meta-schema extract sqlserver Extract SQL Server schema into a MetaSchema workspace.
meta-schema help Show help.
meta-schema extract sqlserver Extract SQL Server schema into a MetaSchema workspace. 7 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-schema extract sqlserver [--all-schemas] [--all-tables] --connection-env <value> --new-workspace <path> [--schema <value>] --system <value> [--table <value>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--all-schemas Extract all SQL Server schemas in scope. False
--all-tables Extract all SQL Server tables and views in scope. False
--connection-env <value> Environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string. <value> False
--new-workspace <path> Directory where the MetaSchema workspace will be created. <path> False
--schema <value> Extract one SQL Server schema. <value> False
--system <value> Source system name recorded in the workspace. <value> False
--table <value> Extract one SQL Server table or view. <value> False
meta-schema help Show help. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-schema help

General examples

Extract a source contract

Extract the selected source boundary before designing downstream workspaces.

Capture the source schema once, then use the resulting workspace as the contract for transform binding and downstream design.

meta-schema extract sqlserver --new-workspace .\SourceSchema.Workspace --connection-env META_SOURCE_SQL --system SourceWarehouse --schema dbo --all-tables
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-sql

Extract SQL Server metadata, build deploy plans, apply manifests, and execute SQL against modeled database workspaces.

Working with MetaSql

MetaSql represents a database and its deployable SQL structure as a modeled workspace. Tables, views, procedures, functions, dependencies, and deployment evidence are available as data that can be inspected, compared, generated, and consumed by other models.

Authoring and deployment have distinct responsibilities. A deploy manifest records the source and live fingerprints that must agree before SQL is applied, turning release readiness into an explicit modeled decision. Generated SQL therefore has a traceable source workspace and a clear contract for the database it is about to change.

MetaSql is the physical SQL boundary for sanctioned converters and generated assets. It provides a natural database-facing representation while staying connected to the source schema, transform, vault, warehouse, or data-quality model that gave the SQL its meaning.

Commands

5 commands exposed by meta-sql.

CommandSummary
meta-sql deploy Apply a deploy manifest after source and live fingerprint validation.
meta-sql deploy-plan Create a deploy manifest from a source MetaSql workspace and a live SQL Server database.
meta-sql execute Execute a SQL Server file or query for demo, bootstrap, and verification scripts.
meta-sql extract sqlserver Extract SQL Server database objects into a MetaSql workspace.
meta-sql help Show help.
meta-sql deploy Apply a deploy manifest after source and live fingerprint validation. 3 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-sql deploy --connection-env <value> --manifest-workspace <path> --source-workspace <path>

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--connection-env <value> Environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string. <value> False
--manifest-workspace <path> Deploy manifest workspace created by deploy-plan. <path> False
--source-workspace <path> Source MetaSql workspace used to create the manifest. <path> False
meta-sql deploy-plan Create a deploy manifest from a source MetaSql workspace and a live SQL Server database. 7 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-sql deploy-plan [--approval-file <path>] [--approve-drop-column <value>] [--approve-drop-table <value>] [--approve-truncate-column <value>] --connection-env <value> --out <path> --source-workspace <path>

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--approval-file <path> JSON file containing destructive approvals. <path> False
--approve-drop-column <value> Approve one exact destructive column drop in the form schema.table.column. <value> False
--approve-drop-table <value> Approve one exact destructive table drop in the form schema.table. <value> False
--approve-truncate-column <value> Approve one exact destructive column truncation in the form schema.table.column. <value> False
--connection-env <value> Environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string. <value> False
--out <path> Directory where the deploy manifest workspace will be written. <path> False
--source-workspace <path> Source MetaSql workspace to compare with the live database. <path> False
meta-sql execute Execute a SQL Server file or query for demo, bootstrap, and verification scripts. 6 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-sql execute --connection-env <value> [--file <path>] [--query <value>] [--quiet] [--timeout-seconds <value>] [--var <value>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--connection-env <value> Environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string. <value> False
--file <path> Execute SQL from a file. Mutually exclusive with --query. <path> False
--query <value> Execute inline SQL. Mutually exclusive with --file. <value> False
--quiet Suppress result-set and success output. False
--timeout-seconds <value> Command timeout for each SQL batch. 0 or omitted means no timeout. <value> False
--var <value> Replace one SQLCMD-style $(NAME) token before execution. <value> False
meta-sql extract sqlserver Extract SQL Server database objects into a MetaSql workspace. 9 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-sql extract sqlserver [--allow-empty] --connection-env <value> [--include-functions] [--include-stored-procedures] [--include-tables] [--include-views] --new-workspace <path> [--schema <value>] [--table <value>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--allow-empty Create an empty database/schema workspace when no tables or modules match. False
--connection-env <value> Environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string. <value> False
--include-functions Extract function modules. If any include switch is provided, only selected object kinds are extracted. False
--include-stored-procedures Extract stored procedure modules. If any include switch is provided, only selected object kinds are extracted. False
--include-tables Extract tables, columns, keys, and indexes. If no include switch is provided, all object kinds are extracted. False
--include-views Extract view modules. If any include switch is provided, only selected object kinds are extracted. False
--new-workspace <path> Directory where the MetaSql workspace will be created. <path> False
--schema <value> Extract only one SQL Server schema. <value> False
--table <value> Extract only one table. SQL module extraction is skipped when a table filter is used. <value> False
meta-sql help Show help. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-sql help

General examples

Apply a verified deployment

Apply a deploy manifest against the source workspace it was created from.

The manifest carries the source and live fingerprint evidence used to decide whether deployment can proceed. The connection option names the environment variable containing the SQL Server connection string.

meta-sql deploy --source-workspace .\MetaSql.Workspace --manifest-workspace .\DeployPlan.Workspace --connection-env META_TARGET_SQL
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-tabular

Author and deploy tabular semantic models with tables, relationships, measures, partitions, roles, and processing commands.

Working with MetaTabular

MetaTabular models a deployable Tabular target without reducing it to an opaque project. Tables, columns, measures, relationships, partitions, calculation groups, roles, and processing choices are ordinary model structures that can be inspected and evolved before they reach Analysis Services.

A Tabular workspace can be authored directly or generated from MetaAnalytics. In either route, the portable semantic contract remains visible and Tabular-specific decisions have a proper modeled home: import mode, partition expressions, calculation-item logic, role filters, and deployment behavior.

Deployment applies the modeled target to an Analysis Services server. That makes the deployed model a result of a reviewable workspace, while still supporting the capabilities expected of a serious Tabular implementation.

Meta-BI / CLI

meta-transform-binding

Bind transform scripts to source and target schemas so references, data types, and writable contracts are verified before generation.

Working with MetaTransformBinding

MetaTransformBinding validates transform scripts against explicit source and target MetaSchema contracts. It resolves referenced objects and columns, verifies target write shape, evaluates data-type compatibility through the sanctioned conversion workspace, and records the resulting evidence in a dedicated binding workspace.

A successful binding says more than that the SQL parsed. It establishes that each modeled source and target reference resolves against the stated schemas and that the transform's write contract is compatible with its target. The recorded rowsets, columns, and validation links become a durable technical basis for later pipeline, orchestration, and quality work.

Binding is an atomic contract gate in normal operation. Its explicit partial mode is for corpus discovery: it keeps the successfully bound scripts while preserving a report of the scripts that need more source, target, or type information.

Commands

2 commands exposed by meta-transform-binding.

CommandSummary
meta-transform-binding bind Bind transform scripts and validate them against source and target schema contracts into a new workspace.
meta-transform-binding help Show help.
meta-transform-binding bind Bind transform scripts and validate them against source and target schema contracts into a new workspace. 11 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-transform-binding bind [--allow-partial] [--data-type-conversion-workspace <path>] --execute-system <value> [--execute-system-default-schema-name <value>] [--ignore-target-columns <col[,col...]>] [--ignore-target-columns-if-present <col[,col...]>] --new-workspace <path> [--partial-report <path>] --source-schema <path> --target-schema <path> --transform-workspace <path>

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--allow-partial Save only objects that bind and validate successfully. False
--data-type-conversion-workspace <path> Optional sanctioned conversion policy workspace. Omitted uses built-in defaults. <path> False
--execute-system <value> Execution context for one/two-part source identifiers. <value> False
--execute-system-default-schema-name <value> Required when any one-part source identifier exists. <value> True
--ignore-target-columns <col[,col...]> Comma-separated target columns to exclude from target conformance checks. <col[,col...]> False
--ignore-target-columns-if-present <col[,col...]> Comma-separated target columns to exclude only on target tables where they exist. <col[,col...]> False
--new-workspace <path> Directory where the binding workspace will be created. <path> False
--partial-report <path> TSV report for objects skipped due to binding or validation failure. Requires --allow-partial. <path> False
--source-schema <path> Repeatable source MetaSchema workspace. <path> False
--target-schema <path> Target MetaSchema workspace. <path> False
--transform-workspace <path> MetaTransformScript workspace to bind. <path> False
meta-transform-binding help Show help. 0 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-transform-binding help

General examples

Validate a transform contract

Bind transform scripts to explicit source and target schemas.

Binding records the resolved source and target evidence only when every required reference and type rule can be proven from the supplied workspaces.

meta-transform-binding bind --transform-workspace .\Transforms.Workspace --source-schema .\SourceSchema.Workspace --target-schema .\WarehouseSchema.Workspace --execute-system SourceWarehouse --execute-system-default-schema-name dbo --new-workspace .\Binding.Workspace
Meta-BI / CLI

meta-transform-script

Import SQL transform scripts into a structured model, export equivalent SQL, and manage transform contracts.

Working with MetaTransformScript

MetaTransformScript understands the supported SQL Server transform surface structurally. It imports views, query expressions, bounded mutation statements, inline table-valued functions, and constrained scalar functions into a typed workspace, then emits semantically equivalent SQL.

The workspace captures the parts of SQL that downstream tools need to reason about: statement ownership, identifiers, expressions, joins, common table expressions, predicates, grouping, windowing, type references, and source paths. A transform can therefore be inspected, bound to schemas, compared after a round-trip, and used as evidence by pipeline, orchestration, and data-quality tools.

The target is faithful syntax modeling. SQL is represented once as structured data, not reduced to a blob or replaced by a guessed lineage graph. Dynamic and procedural forms remain clearly bounded, while the supported surface can be proven through SQL-to-model-to-SQL equivalence and workspace diff evidence.

Commands

10 commands exposed by meta-transform-script.

CommandSummary
meta-transform-script from sql-code Import SQL text.
meta-transform-script from sql-file Import one .sql file.
meta-transform-script from sql-files Import many .sql files from an explicit manifest.
meta-transform-script help Show help.
meta-transform-script stored-procedure add-contract Add or replace stored procedure contract metadata.
meta-transform-script stored-procedure remove-contract Remove a stored procedure contract and its declared rows.
meta-transform-script stored-procedure view-contract View stored procedure contracts.
meta-transform-script target-identifiers from-pattern Derive view target identifiers from transform script names.
meta-transform-script to sql-code Emit one transform script as SQL text.
meta-transform-script to sql-path Emit SQL scripts to a file or folder.
meta-transform-script from sql-code Import SQL text. 5 options / 0 examples

Usage

meta-transform-script from sql-code --code <sql> [--name <value>] [--new-workspace <path>] [--target <sql-identifier>] [--workspace <path>]

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired
--code <sql> SQL text to import. <sql> False
--name <value> Required when SQL code is bare SELECT or mutation text without a CREATE wrapper. <value> True
--new-workspace <path> Create a new MetaTransformScript workspace. <path> False
--target <sql-identifier> Optional for CREATE VIEW imports; required for bare SELECT; not allowed for inline CREATE FUNCTION or mutation imports. <sql-identifier> False
--workspace <path> Add one script to an existing workspace. <path> False
meta-transform-script from sql-file Import one .sql file. 4 options / 0 examples

Options

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

--target-pattern <pattern> Pattern rendered into TargetSqlIdentifier from captured source tokens. <pattern> False

OptionDescriptionValueRequired

General examples

Round-trip a transform view

Import a view into a typed transform workspace and emit it again as SQL.

The workspace captures the view structure once. Exported SQL can be compared with the source artifact or bound to an explicit schema contract.

meta-transform-script from sql-file --path .\views\v_customer.sql --new-workspace .\Transforms.Workspace --target dbo.v_customer
meta-transform-script to sql-path --workspace .\Transforms.Workspace --out .\RoundTrippedSql
